generations.text:
**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you far from the comforts of home. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and with each passing day, the silence between us grows heavier, like the weight of the sea upon the rocks. I write to you now not only as a father, but as a man who has spent his life standing guard over this lighthouse, watching the waves and the winds, and now, I find myself facing a storm that may take more than just my life.

The sky has darkened with an ominous promise, and the wind howls like a wounded beast, rattling the very bones of this old tower. I have seen storms before, many of them, but this one feels different—more fierce, more relentless. The sea, which has always been my companion, now roars with fury, and I fear that I may not be able to keep it at bay. I have tended to the light for many years, and I know the signs of a coming tempest, but I have never felt such a deep, gnawing fear as I do now.

You were the light in my life, Eleanor, the one who brought me joy and warmth when the world seemed to be turning cold. I remember the days we spent together, when the lighthouse was not just a place of duty but a home filled with laughter and love. I remember your laughter echoing through the halls

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the stone walls of this old lighthouse, as though the very earth is crying out in anguish. The sea, once a faithful companion, now roars with fury, and the storm that is to come tonight is not one I have seen in many a year. I fear the night may take more than just my life—it may take the last of my courage, and perhaps even the last of my hope.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, weary years since the last time we spoke. I have not written to you, nor have I dared to call you. The silence between us has grown like the weeds that creep through the cracks in the old stone, stubborn and unyielding. I have tried, in my heart, to find the words that might mend what has been broken, but the words elude me, as though they were never truly mine to give.

I know you have been gone from this place, from this life, and I have not been the father you needed. I have been a shadow, a man lost to the sea and the ghosts of my own making. I have not been there for you, and for that, I am truly sorry. I pray that, in the quiet of this storm, the weight of my silence may find its way to you, and that you might forgive me.

Tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I feel

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink of a man who has spent more years gazing at the sea than at the faces of those he loves. The storm is upon us, and the wind howls like a banshee, as if the very heavens are weeping for what has been lost between us. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear that when this night is done, I may never have the chance again.

You were twelve when you left, and I remember the way your small hands clutched the doorframe as you stood on the threshold of our home. Your mother’s voice was soft, pleading, and I could not bear to see the pain in your eyes. I told you to go, Eleanor. I told you to leave, and I have never regretted it—not truly. But now, as the waves crash against the cliffs and the wind screams through the cracks of this old lighthouse, I find myself wondering if I was wrong.

The sea has always been my companion, my guide, my curse. It has shown me the beauty of the world, but also its cruelty. And now, as the storm gathers strength, I am reminded of the night you left. The night the light went out, and I could not find the strength to reach for you.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. Even when you were gone, you remained in my thoughts, in the flicker of the beacon

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lighthouse. The sea roars in protest, and the sky is bruised with clouds that threaten to swallow the moon whole. I do not know if I shall live to see dawn, but if I do, I must speak now—before the storm claims me and before the silence between us becomes eternal.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have counted every day like a candle burning down to its last ember. You were once my light, Eleanor—bright, fierce, and unyielding. Now, you are but a shadow in my memory, a ghost I cannot quite banish. I have not spoken your name in so long, and yet it lingers in my throat like a stone.

I have kept the light burning, as I have always done. It is my duty, my oath—to guide the lost, to keep the sea from swallowing the unwary. But tonight, I fear the sea may swallow me. The storm is unlike any I have seen, and the waves rise like the wrath of some ancient god. I have not slept in days, watching the horizon with a heart that feels heavier than the stones beneath my feet.

You once asked me why I never told you the truth about your mother’s passing. I was afraid. Afraid that knowing would break you, as it broke me. But now, as

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy as the storm that gathers beyond the cliffs. Five years have passed since your last letter, and though I have kept the lighthouse burning every night, I have kept my silence as well. I have not spoken to you, not once, and I fear I may never again. But tonight—tonight, the night of the storm—I find myself compelled to reach out, not to beg for your forgiveness, but to speak before the sea takes me and the last words between us are lost to the waves.

You were never meant to be a daughter to me, Eleanor. Not in the way you were to your mother, nor in the way I once dreamed you would be. You were a child of the city, of fine parlors and polished manners, and I, a man of the sea and the stone. We were not meant to be. I tried, I truly did, to make you see the world as I do—where the light must be kept, where the horizon is our only companion, where the wind speaks louder than words. But you were not made for that life. You were made for the drawing rooms of the gentry, for silk and silver, for the comforts of a world that does not understand the weight of duty.

And yet, I cannot deny that I have missed you. Not in the way a man might miss a son, or a wife, but in the quiet

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the ghosts of my own regrets. The sea is rising, dark and furious, and I fear the night may break the lighthouse itself. I have not spoken to you in five years, not since the day you left, and yet here I am, still waiting for you to return.

I know you think me a cruel man, that I cast you out like a dog. But I did not do it out of spite, nor out of indifference. I did it because I could not bear to see you hurt again. You were so young when you left, barely more than a child, and I saw the fire in your eyes — the fire of ambition, of defiance, of a will that would not be bent. You wanted more than this life of salt and stone and endless nights. And I, in my foolish pride, thought I could hold you back.

But I was wrong.

You were right to go. You were right to chase the horizon, to seek your own path. And I, in my stubbornness, let pride blind me to the truth: you were never meant to stay here, bound to this rock and this light.

I have not written to you because I feared what I might say. I feared I would beg you to come back, and that you would refuse. I feared I would be forced to admit that I have always loved you, and that I have

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It has been five years since I last laid eyes upon you, and yet the weight of that silence still sits heavy upon my soul. I write this with a quill that trembles, not from the cold, but from the storm that now brews beyond the horizon—both in the skies and in my heart.

I have spent these years in the solitude of this lighthouse, tending to the flame that guides the lost and the weary. The sea has been my companion, and the wind has whispered tales of your life to me in the dead of night. I have heard of your marriage, your children, the life you have built far from this lonely shore. I have heard of your laughter, your sorrow, and the way the world moves on without me. And yet, I have said nothing.

The reason for our estrangement is not one I can easily put to paper. It is not a matter of words, but of wounds that time has not healed. I have tried, Eleanor, I have tried to reach out, to mend what was broken, but you have turned away. Perhaps you thought I had no right to ask for your forgiveness, or perhaps you believed I had no right to be here at all.

But tonight, as the sky darkens and the winds howl with a fury that even the sea cannot contain, I find myself questioning whether I will see another dawn. The storm is unlike any I have seen in my years

**April 12th, 1887**  
**To My Dearest Eleanor,**  

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with a fury I have never known. The lighthouse stands, though I fear it may not for long. I’ve spent the last three hours securing the lamp and the beacon, and the storm shows no sign of abating. The sky is a mass of black clouds, and the waves crash against the cliffs with such force that I fear the rocks may give way beneath them.  

I’ve not written to you in five years, Eleanor. I know you’ve been angry, and I deserve it. I was a fool to let pride and duty blind me to the truth of what I was doing. I should have been there for you, not chasing the illusion of duty. I should have known you would not forgive me.  

I still remember the day you left. You were so angry, so heartbroken. I could see the pain in your eyes, and I could not bear to meet it. I told you I had to stay here, that the lighthouse was my responsibility, that without me, the coast would be lost. You said I was a coward, that I had chosen the sea over your love. And I did not deny it.  

But now, as the storm rages, I wonder if I made the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a wounded beast, rattling the windows and clawing at the very stones of this old lighthouse. The sea roars with a fury that seems almost human, as if it knows the hour is near and the world is about to be remade. I have kept this lamp burning all night, though I fear it may not be enough. The storm is coming, and I am not sure I will see dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years of watching the horizon, of hearing the waves but never your voice. I have not spoken to you in that time, not even in the letters I once sent—letters that were never answered, and I suppose you never received. Perhaps they were lost in the sea, or perhaps you chose to forget them. I cannot say.

You were always a spirited child, full of fire and defiance. I remember the day you ran from the house, your cloak flapping like the wings of a bird, your eyes full of something that was not fear, but something else. I did not understand it then, and I still do not. But I have come to believe that you chose your own path, and I, in my stubbornness, chose to stay behind. To watch. To keep the light burning for those who might need it. To be a man of the

**The Lighthouse Keeper's Letter**  
*Written in the hand of a man who has seen too much, and loved too much, to let it go.*

---

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a wounded beast outside, and the sea roiling as if it would swallow the very rocks upon which I stand. The lantern is dim, the oil is low, and the storm—*the storm*—is coming. I do not know if I shall see the dawn, and if I do, I do not know if I shall see you again.

Five years. Five long, bitter years since you left. I have not heard a word from you, not a single letter, not a whisper of your voice. I have not seen your face, nor felt your hand, nor heard your laughter. It is as if you have been swallowed by the sea, or perhaps by time itself.

I have not spoken of you to anyone. Not to the other keepers, not to the village folk, not even to the ghost of your mother, who once used to sit with me and speak of you in hushed tones. Now she is gone, and I am alone with the wind and the waves and my own bitter thoughts.

I have always believed in duty, Eleanor. I have always believed in the light, in the work, in the sacred duty of a lighthouse keeper. But I have never believed in silence. I have never

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The sea has been restless these past few nights, and the wind howls like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lighthouse. I fear the storm that gathers on the horizon may be the end of me. But I must write, for I cannot bear the thought of leaving this world without one final word to you.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have spent them in quiet despair, watching the waves and wondering if you ever thought of me at all. You were always so certain of your path, so certain of your purpose. You spoke of the world beyond these cliffs, of grand cities and distant lands, of a life that would not be bound by the tides and the salt. And I, foolish man that I am, allowed you to go, believing that you would return.

But you did not. And I have not spoken a word to you in all this time. I have not written, nor have I sent a single letter. I have only kept the light burning, as I always have, and prayed that the world would not forget the lighthouse, nor the man who guards it.

Now, the sky is dark with storm clouds, and the sea roars with fury. I can feel the wind pressing against the windows, as though the very elements are trying to tear this place apart. I do not know if I will live

**April 12th, 1897**  
**St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isle of Wight**  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

I write to you now, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars in protest against the heavens. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may be the last I shall see of this world. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as the darkness gathers, I find myself thinking of you more than ever. Perhaps it is the loneliness of the lighthouse, or perhaps it is the weight of years unspoken that draws me back to the only person I ever truly loved.  

You were but a child when you left, and I was a man who had lost his way. I had thought that by letting you go, I was sparing you from the sorrow I carried. But I was wrong, Eleanor. I was only sparing myself from the burden of watching you grow up without me. I did not understand then that love is not a thing to be given or taken, but a thing that must be tended like a flame—left to die, or kept alight with care.  

I have not written to you, nor have I sent a single letter, for I feared the words would not be enough. I feared that even if I spoke, I would not be able to explain the things I should have said long ago. But tonight, as the storm

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay you may not believe it. I write to you from the solitude of the lighthouse, where the sea roars like a beast in its wrath, and the wind howls with a voice that echoes through the very stones of this old place. It has been five long years since our last conversation, and I have often wondered if you still remember the sound of my voice—or if it has been lost to the tides of time.

Tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself gripped by a fear that has long lain dormant. The skies have darkened with a fury that has not been seen in years, and the waves, with their relentless might, threaten to devour the very foundations of this lighthouse. I have spent my life guarding these shores, and yet, for the first time in my years of service, I am not certain I will be able to stand against the tempest that is coming.

I know you have your own life to live, far from the desolation of this coast. You have chosen a path of your own, and I have not sought to interfere. But I cannot help but feel the weight of my years pressing upon me, and the thought of leaving this place—this home—without the knowledge that you are safe and well is a burden I cannot bear.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. Even now, as I sit

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and the weight of that silence has grown heavier with each passing day. I have often wondered what you might have become, what life has brought you, and if you still remember the girl who once ran through the dunes with me, her laughter echoing like the waves on the shore.

Tonight, a storm is coming—great and terrible, as though the very heavens are weeping. The winds howl like a beast, and the sea roars with fury. I have stood at the edge of the cliff, the lighthouse towering behind me, and I have felt the pull of the sea as if it knows my time is drawing near. I do not know if I shall survive it, Eliza. The tempest has been a constant companion these past weeks, and I have grown weary.

I have not spoken to you in years, and I do not know if you will even read these words. But I must try. I have spent many nights staring into the flames of the lamp, wondering if I have done enough to keep the light shining for those who sail the dark waters. I have done my duty, as I always have, but I have also felt the loneliness of this place, the silence of the sea, and the absence of your voice.

I have heard stories of you, of course. The village

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these ancient walls. The sea roars, and the storm is upon us—God help us all. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as I sit here by the light, I find myself thinking of you more than ever.

You were a child when you left, with that stubborn pride of yours. I remember the day you stormed out, your eyes burning with anger and hurt. You said I was too strict, too rigid, too much like the lighthouse itself—unyielding, unfeeling. I did not argue. I simply stood at the door, watching the rain fall, and let you go.

But I have not forgotten. Not a single day. I have kept your things, Eliza—your little bonnet, your favorite shawl, the book of poems you used to read by the fire. They sit on the shelf above the lamp, where the light never fails to reach them. I told myself I was doing this for your sake, that you needed to grow, to find your own path. But I have also been doing it for mine. For the silence, the loneliness, the quiet ache of a father’s love that has no place to go.

Tonight, the storm is unlike any I have seen in my years here. The waves are rising, the wind is tearing at the shutters, and

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as if it knows the hour is late. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to write. Not because I believe I shall survive this night, but because I fear I may not, and I would not have the chance to say what I must.

You were once my pride, my light in the dark. I remember the day you left—your eyes filled with fire, your voice sharp with words I could not unhear. You called me a fool, a relic of a bygone age, and I let you go. I did not argue, for I knew you were right. You had your dreams, your ambitions, your future. I clung to the lighthouse, to the rhythm of the tides, to the constancy of the sea. And you left me behind.

But now, as the storm gathers, I find myself thinking of you more than I ever have. The light from the lighthouse flickers, as if uncertain whether to continue. I have not been able to keep it burning for days. I have grown tired, my bones aching with the weight of years. The wind has taken much from me—my strength, my hope, even my voice. And yet, I still stand

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I dares not hope for much these days. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I have been a fool to let silence grow between us like a weed in the garden. I write to you now not from the comfort of my study, but from the cold, windswept tower where I have spent my twilight years, watching the sea with a heart heavier than the storm that now approaches.

The sky is dark as a widow’s shawl, and the wind howls like a banshee. The sea, once a patient companion, now rages with a fury that makes me fear for my life. I have seen worse storms, but never with the weight of a father’s regret pressing down upon my chest. I do not know if I will survive this night, and if I do, I do not know what I will be left with—save the memories of you, my dear Eleanor.

I have often wondered what you might have become. Did you take after your mother’s quiet strength, or did you follow your father’s stubborn path? I know not. You were always a puzzle, and I, a man too proud to ask the questions that would have brought us closer. I should have been there when you needed me, not absent as a ghost in your life. I should have held you when the world felt too cruel, not left you to face it alone.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these walls. The sea is restless tonight, churning with fury, and I fear the tempest that lies ahead may be more than nature can muster. I have kept this lighthouse for thirty years, and never before have I felt the weight of the storm as I do now. But I fear that this time, it may be my last.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years. I have tried to forget, to move on, to find solace in the rhythm of the waves and the quiet of this place. But the silence is deafening, and the nights are filled with your absence. I have not heard your voice in years, nor seen your face, and yet I feel your shadow in every gust of wind, every crashing wave.

I know you believe me to be a cruel man, that I turned my back on you when you needed me most. But I was not cruel. I was broken. I was a man who had lost everything—my wife, my son, and then you. I tried to reach out, to make amends, but you would not listen. You left, and I was left to pick up the pieces of a life that had long since crumbled beneath me.

I have no words to explain the pain of that night, nor the guilt that has haunted me ever since. I only

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I fear it may not find you at all. The winds have been rising all night, and the sea, as always, is restless. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, and the light cuts through the gathering storm like a blade. I write to you now, not with the hope that you will read it, but with the hope that if you do, you might understand.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since you walked out of this house, with your father's coat still on your shoulders, and your eyes full of fire. I do not know if you remember the night you left, or if you remember me at all. I do not know if you still think of me as your father, or if you have long since forgotten the man I once was.

But I write to you now because the storm is coming, and I am not sure I will see the dawn. I have been a lighthouse keeper for thirty years, and I have seen many storms. I have seen the sea rage and the sky darken, and I have known the weight of the world pressing in on me. But this storm—this one—feels different. It is not just the wind or the waves that trouble me, but the silence. The silence of your absence, the silence of my own heart.

You were always the light in this house,

**April 12th, 1897**  
**To My Dearest Eleanor,**  

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee just beyond the lighthouse walls. The sea is restless tonight, and the sky is a canvas of black and silver, as if the heavens themselves are weeping. I fear we may not see dawn.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and five years have done little to mend the rift between us. I have tried, Eleanor, I have truly tried. I wrote to you often, though I know you never answered. I left letters on the shore, tied with ribbons, hoping the tide might carry them to you. But you have not come back. You have not even sent a single word.

I used to think it was the sea that had taken you. That some cruel wave had swept you away, and that I would never see you again. But now, as I sit here, the storm gathering strength, I wonder if it was not the sea, but the silence. The silence of your absence. The silence of your heart.

You were always a brave girl, Eleanor. I remember the day you first came to the lighthouse, when you were but a child, your eyes wide with wonder as you looked up at the great beacon. You asked me if I ever felt lonely, and I told you that loneliness was a companion I had learned to live with. But you

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now by the flickering light of the oil lamp, the sea howling like a wounded beast just beyond the stone walls of this lonely keep. The wind has been rising all day, and the waves have grown restless, as though the ocean itself foresees what is to come. I have been watching the sky for hours, and I fear the storm that approaches will not be kind to those who dwell in its path.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not dared to write, fearing the weight of my words might crush you as surely as the sea might crush this lighthouse. I have been a fool, Eliza. A man who let pride and silence grow between us like weeds in the cracks of a crumbling wall. I do not know if you still remember me, or if you have long since forgotten the man I once was. I pray you do not.

You were always my light, my guiding star. I would have given my life to protect you from the darkness of the world, but I failed you. I let you go, and I have never truly found my way back to you. I have spent these years in this isolation, with only the sea and the ever-watchful eyes of the stars for company. I have not been the father you deserved, and I have not been the man you once loved.

Tonight, the storm is upon us. The wind screams through the cracks,

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 3rd, 1887**  
**Lighthouse, Isle of Wight**

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, for my fingers are numb from the cold, and my heart is heavier than the sea that roars outside these walls. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves crash against the cliff with a fury that makes me fear I may not live to see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left this house, Eleanor. Five years since you walked out with your head held high and your eyes filled with fire. I have not heard a word from you, not a single letter, not a single word of comfort. You have turned your back on your father, and I have turned my back on you. But tonight—tonight, I find myself writing to you. Not because I have found peace, but because I fear I may never see you again.

I have always been the keeper of the light, you know. Not just the light that guides the ships through the storm, but the light that keeps the darkness at bay. I have stood here for over thirty years, watching the tides, the stars, the endless horizon. I have seen countless ships pass, and I have seen many disappear into the night. But none have ever haunted me as much as the silence of your absence.

You were once my pride, my only child, the light

The sea roars in fury, and I can feel the very stones beneath me shudder as if the earth itself is weeping. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as this storm gathers, I find myself longing for your voice more than ever.

I know you do not wish to hear from me. You left, as you always have, and I have never found the strength to chase you back. But tonight is different. Tonight, I feel the weight of my years pressing down upon me like the tide, and I fear that this storm may claim me before the dawn.

You were always the strong one, Eleanor. I remember the day you first came to me with your father’s telescope, your eyes alight with the fire of discovery. You were young then, full of questions and dreams. I tried to nurture that fire, but it burned too brightly for a man like me—old, set in my ways, and too proud to ask for help. I let my pride blind me, and in doing so, I lost you.

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy as the storm clouds that now gather over the cliffs. The sea howls like a beast in pain, and the wind screams as though it knows the sorrow I carry. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the heavens weep and the waves crash with a fury that shakes the very bones of this old lighthouse, I feel the need to speak—though to whom, I do not know.

I have always been a man of the sea, Eleanor. I have known its moods, its tides, its cruel and tender ways. But I have never known such a storm as this. The sky is black as ink, and the wind carries with it the scent of salt and death. I fear the light may not hold through the night.

You were a child when you left, and I have watched the years pass like the slow turning of the great clock in the tower. I have not heard your voice in all that time, not even in the dreams that haunt me. You were my light, my guide, my only hope in the endless dark. And now, I fear I have become the shadow you fled.

As the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars with a fury that has not been seen in these parts for many a year, I find myself penned in the dim glow of the lighthouse, surrounded by the familiar creaks and groans of our old structure. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than just the light from our tower. For five long years, I have carried the weight of silence between us, and now, as the tempest brews, I feel the need to speak—though I do not know if my words will reach you, or if they will be lost in the chaos of the night.

You were once my greatest joy, my little light in the dark, and I have often wondered what became of you. The world has changed since you left, and I have found myself adrift in a sea of memories, longing for the days when our laughter echoed through the halls of our home. I remember the way you would sit by the hearth, your eyes alight with curiosity, asking questions that made me pause and ponder. You were a child of wonder, and I, a man who had long since forgotten how to dream.

But time has a way of eroding even the strongest bonds. I know I have been a poor father, too caught up in the duties of this lighthouse, too consumed by the sea to see the love I once had for

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now, with the wind howling like a banshee and the sea roaring as if it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. The night is thick with the scent of salt and fear, and I find myself gripping the lantern so tightly that my fingers ache. This is no ordinary night, Eliza. **My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink trembling in my hand, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the narrow cracks of this old lighthouse. The sea is restless, the sky a bruised canvas of black and silver, and I fear the night may claim me before dawn. But if I do, I pray this letter finds you — not as a plea for forgiveness, but as a final attempt to mend what time and silence have broken.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five years of hollow evenings, of the lamp’s glow casting long shadows on the walls, and of the sea’s ceaseless, accusing whispers. I have not spoken your name in all that time. I have not spoken to you. And yet, tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in years.

I know what you think of me — that I was too proud, too stubborn, too afraid to let you go. That I let the sea take you from me. But I did not. I let *myself* take you from me. I chose to hold on, not because I loved you, but because I feared what I would become without you. I feared the silence, the loneliness, the hollow echo of a home that no longer felt like home. And so I shut you out, not with words, but with silence. I am sorry, Eleanor. I am so sorry.
